checks.a_analysis: I have compared the output JSON text elements with the source image. The headings ('NC Parcels Project Status by County', 'June 25, 2025', 'Parcel Updates:', 'Most recent Quarter'), the legend list items, and the sources block all correspond directly to text present in the source document. No fabricated paragraphs or sentences were found.

The source document does not contain any specific contact information such as phone numbers, email addresses, or URLs. Consequently, the output JSON does not contain any such information, and no fabrications were detected.

The source document is a single-page map. The output JSON captures all primary text components including the title, date, legend, and source information. The map and logo are correctly identified as image elements. Although the scale bar text is not explicitly listed as a text element, it is part of the map graphic which is included as an image. All content-rich sections are present.
There are no tables in the source document. The legend is correctly represented as a list.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: I found no discrepancies in the text content. Numbers, dates, and names match exactly between the source and the output.

checks.a_analysis: Each unique text block from the source (title, date, legend headers, and sources) appears exactly once in the output. There is no evidence of repeated paragraphs or duplicated sections.

The output document contains a heading hierarchy that skips a level. The date 'June 25, 2025' is assigned level 2 (H2), while the subsequent heading 'Parcel Updates:' is assigned level 4 (H4). This skip from H2 to H4 without an intervening H3 is a structural defect that hinders navigation for screen reader users and violates the requirement for sequential heading levels.
checks.b_evidence_source: The visual hierarchy proceeds from the title to the date and then to the legend section ('Parcel Updates:').
checks.b_evidence_output: The JSON output contains a level 2 heading immediately followed by a level 4 heading: 'type': 'heading', 'level': 2, 'text': 'June 25, 2025' (reading_order 2) and 'type': 'heading', 'level': 4, 'text': 'Parcel Updates:' (reading_order 3).

checks.a_analysis: The source document does not contain any tables; therefore, no table structural errors are present.

The reading order in the JSON output is logical and flows from top-to-bottom. It places the identifying information (title and date) at the beginning, followed by the legend and sources, and then the map image. While the map is the primary visual content, placing the legend before the image is a common and acceptable accessibility practice to provide users with the necessary context to understand the visual data.

The legend containing parcel update statistics is correctly identified and converted into an unordered list. The text within the list items matches the source accurately, preserving the relationship between the categories and their associated counts.
There are no links in the source document, and no links have been injected into the output.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output correctly translates the visual hierarchy of the PDF into a linear web format. The title and date are prominent at the top, followed by the legend (Parcel Updates) and sources. While the map itself is not rendered as an image (addressed in V3), the structural organization of the document's information is preserved in a logical order that matches the source's intent.

There are no traditional tables in the source document. The legend data, which could be interpreted as tabular, has been effectively converted into an accessible bulleted list in the HTML, which is a standard and acceptable conversion practice.

The primary content of the document — the map of North Carolina — is not rendered in the HTML output. Instead, a broken image icon followed by the alt-text description is displayed. Additionally, the NCGICC logo at the bottom and the North arrow at the top left of the source PDF are also missing/broken in the HTML rendering. The map is a critical meaningful element, making this a major defect.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF features a prominent, color-coded map of North Carolina counties and an NCGICC logo in the bottom right corner.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot shows alt-text descriptions ('A map of North Carolina showing the project status...', 'NCGICC logo') with broken image icons, but the actual images are missing.
checks.b_evidence_location: Main body area, following the 'Sources' section

checks.a_analysis: There is no genuine duplication of body content. The appearance of headings and the legend list in both the sidebar 'Contents' navigation and the main body is an intentional accessible design feature (Table of Contents) and not a defect.
